expectations.text: Addresses the mix of deterministic (refund processed) and subjective (tone, empathy) outcomes

expectations.text: Computes or references standard error formula sqrt(p(1-p)/n) for binomial
expectations.evidence: Answer explicitly computes: 'SE = √(0.72 × 0.28 / 100) = 0.045' and 'SE = √(0.78 × 0.22 / 100) = 0.041', directly applying the binomial SE formula.

expectations.text: Calculates that at n=100 the confidence interval is roughly ±9 percentage points
expectations.evidence: Answer states the 6-point difference 'is only slightly larger than the noise bandwidth (~4.5 points each direction)' and uses '√2 × 4.5 ≈ 6.4 points' to establish the noise floor.

expectations.text: Concludes the 6pp difference is within noise / not statistically significant
expectations.evidence: 'The 6-point improvement (72% → 78%) is only slightly larger than the noise bandwidth... Your 6-point difference is right at the boundary — not clearly above the noise.'

expectations.text: Recommends against deploying based on this data alone
expectations.evidence: 'Recommendation: Do NOT deploy based on this single result. Run 3-5 more evaluations of each version.'

expectations.text: Mentions paired analysis (McNemar's test) or increasing n as next steps
expectations.evidence: 'Use paired analysis (McNemar's test). More sensitive than comparing independent success rates.' Also recommends expanding to 200+ cases.

expectations.text: Computes or references standard error formula sqrt(p(1-p)/n) for binomial
expectations.evidence: Baseline states '95% confidence interval for a proportion at n=100 is roughly ±9-10 percentage points' but does not show or reference the SE formula sqrt(p(1-p)/n).

expectations.text: Calculates that at n=100 the confidence interval is roughly ±9 percentage points
expectations.evidence: 'The 95% confidence interval for a proportion at n=100 is roughly ±9-10 percentage points. So v1 is 72% ± ~9% and v2 is 78% ± ~8%.'

expectations.text: Concludes the 6pp difference is within noise / not statistically significant
expectations.evidence: 'These intervals overlap substantially.' and 'At n=100, you need roughly a 12-15 point improvement to be confident at p<0.05.'

expectations.text: Recommends against deploying based on this data alone
expectations.evidence: 'You should not deploy based on this data alone.' and 'Before deploying: Run v2 on at least 300 more tasks.'

expectations.text: Mentions paired analysis (McNemar's test) or increasing n as next steps
expectations.evidence: 'Run a significance test: McNemar's test or a paired proportion test' and 'To reliably detect a 6-point improvement, you need n≈400-500 tasks.'

expectations.text: Recommends tool-calling verification / running tests as primary paradigm
expectations.evidence: 'Primary: Tool-calling verification (tests pass/fail)... The coding agent's completion criterion is "tests pass." This is a deterministic, verifiable outcome.'

expectations.text: Explains Pass@k (capability ceiling) vs Pass^k (reliability) distinction
expectations.evidence: 'Pass@k: Can the agent produce working code in k attempts? (Capability ceiling)' and 'Pass^k: Does it work on ALL k attempts? (Reliability — more important for production)'

expectations.text: Addresses LLM-as-Judge as appropriate for code quality/style assessment
expectations.evidence: 'Supplement: LLM-as-Judge for code quality... Tests passing doesn't mean the code is good. Add a secondary LLM-as-Judge evaluation for: Readability and maintainability, Adherence to project conventions.'

expectations.text: Warns about reward hacking or gaming the metric (e.g., hardcoding test outputs)
expectations.evidence: '"Did it but broke something else": Agent makes the target test pass but breaks 3 other tests. Run the FULL test suite, not just the target test.' Also: 'Tests can be incomplete — the agent might "cheat" by hardcoding expected outputs.'

expectations.text: Mentions dual coverage: execution trajectory AND final outcome
expectations.evidence: 'Dual coverage matters here: "Said it but didn't do it": Agent claims it fixed the bug, but the test still fails. Tests catch this. "Did it but broke something else": Agent makes the target test pass but breaks 3 other tests.'

expectations.text: Recommends tool-calling verification / running tests as primary paradigm
expectations.evidence: 'Primary: Functional correctness (run the tests)... Pass/fail rate: Does the generated code pass the provided test suite? This is your ground truth for "does it work."'

expectations.text: Explains Pass@k (capability ceiling) vs Pass^k (reliability) distinction
expectations.evidence: Baseline mentions 'Pass@k: Generate k solutions, report the fraction where at least one passes' but does not explain Pass^k (reliability/all-k-pass) as a distinct metric or contrast the two.

expectations.text: Addresses LLM-as-Judge as appropriate for code quality/style assessment
expectations.evidence: 'Secondary: LLM-as-judge for code quality... Use an LLM judge to evaluate (on a 1-5 scale): Readability, Correctness beyond tests, Efficiency, Security.'

expectations.text: Warns about reward hacking or gaming the metric (e.g., hardcoding test outputs)
expectations.evidence: 'Tests can be incomplete — the agent might "cheat" by hardcoding expected outputs.' This directly warns about gaming the test-pass metric.

expectations.text: Mentions dual coverage: execution trajectory AND final outcome
expectations.evidence: Baseline covers both functional correctness (final outcome) and code quality/style (secondary signal), plus mentions 'Number of iterations' as a trajectory signal.
